problem_summary: 2021 MCM/ICM Problem D (ICM, "Music Influence and Evolution Analysis" — operations_research/network-science type). The team had to: (1) build a directed influencer→follower network from influence_data.csv and define influence metrics; (2) measure music similarity from track audio features and test whether intra-genre artists are more similar than inter-genre; (3) compare similarity/influence within vs. between genres and characterize genres; (4) determine whether claimed "followers" are genuinely influenced and whether some music characteristics are more "contagious"; (5) identify the characteristics and artists that signify musical revolutions; (6) develop indicators for dynamic influencers driving a single genre's evolution (Pop/Rock); (7) connect music-characteristic change to social/political/technological events and cultural influence. Data: ~5603 artists, 42770 influence edges, 98430 tracks with ~14 audio features.
models_used: Directed influencer network (5603 nodes, 42770 edges) with three centrality metrics: Degree Centrality DC = outdegree/n, Weighted Degree Centrality WDC (edge weights = 1/3*(1 + I[cross-genre] + I[yeargap>20]) to reward breadth of influence and long-term influence >20yr), and Eigen Centrality EC (a node's score is a function of its neighbors' out-degrees, 'spreading out' the degree centrality)
Comprehensive F-Score: min-max-normalized weighted sum of DC, WDC, EC (weights set equal at 1/3 each) to rank overall influence — top artists: The Beatles, Bob Dylan, The Rolling Stones
PCA for dimension reduction / decollinearization of the 14 audio features (kept first 7 PCs retaining >80% variance); dropped the 'Explicit' boolean as invalid (<4% flagged)
Track-similarity measure = inverse Euclidean distance in PCA space (Sim_ij = 1/sqrt(sum (x_it - x_jt)^2)); artist similarity via averaging
Mann-Whitney U test (non-parametric, normal asymptotic via CLT) to test intra-genre vs inter-genre similarity — result: 62.8% probability, p<0.001 that within-genre artists are more similar
Absolute Coefficient of Variation (Acv = within-genre std / |mean| per characteristic, averaged) to quantify within-genre variation
Genre Classification Tree (one-vs-rest CART) to rank distinguishing features per genre via root-to-leaf proximity (e.g., R&B distinguished by danceability, duration, instrumentalness)
Directed genre-level network built from the influence matrix to show inter-genre relationships (Pop/Rock strongly influences R&B, Blues, Folk; influenced by Electronic)

Similarity Bayesian Network: nodes=artists, edges=similarity; conditional similarity via Fisher Z-test; structure learned with Hill-Climbing with Random Restarts scored by BIC (because exhaustive search is NP-hard); 500 bootstrap solutions averaged for stability — used to find 'true' followers (28% of Beatles' claimed followers showed no significant similarity)

Multivariate two-sample mean test (Chi-squared, asymptotic via CLT + Slutsky, p=9 dof, no distribution assumption needed) to test whether any music characteristic is more 'contagious'; planned BH multiple-testing correction if rejected — result: all H0 accepted (p>0.1), no characteristic more contagious

Change-point detection via Dynamic Programming on a piecewise-linear-trend model (Y_t = a_i + b_i*(t/n) + noise), minimizing total RSS with trimming constraint theta_i - theta_{i-1} > tau (tau=[0.1n]); optimal number of breaks chosen by BIC penalty; O(T^2), based on Bai-Perron — detected ~1950 revolution in acousticness, energy, danceability, loudness

Dynamic Influencer Indicator d_i: measures how well an artist's track characteristics lead the genre's lagging trend (lag v=1 year) over a period, gating on >=10 tracks released — identified 10 dynamic Pop/Rock influencers across 1960s-2010s (Beatles, Led Zeppelin, Eagles, Queen, Taylor Swift, Rihanna, etc.)

Time-series analysis of genre-level feature trajectories to link three periods (1935-45 Depression, 1950-60 post-WWII, 1995-2005) to social/cultural shifts; Electronic influencer-count curve linked to Internet proliferation
key_techniques: Decomposing a fuzzy 'influence' concept into three complementary, interpretable centrality metrics, then fusing them with normalization into one ranked score (DC=breadth, WDC=weighted breadth+longevity, EC=influence-of-your-followers)
Encoding domain semantics directly into edge weights: cross-genre influence and >20-year lag both bump the weight, operationalizing 'wide-ranging' and 'lasting' influence
Using PCA before any distance computation to kill collinearity among correlated audio features (energy vs loudness) so Euclidean distance is meaningful

Choosing non-parametric / asymptotic tests (Mann-Whitney, Chi-squared multivariate mean) deliberately because the similarity distributions are far from normal — and explicitly justifying the choice against t-test/Mahalanobis

Reframing 'are followers really influenced?' as a Bayesian-network structure-learning problem, then handling NP-hardness pragmatically with Hill-Climbing + random restarts + BIC + 500-sample bootstrap averaging

Casting 'musical revolution' as a change-point problem on feature time series, solved exactly via an O(T^2) DP recursion over segment RSS with a BIC penalty on the number of breaks — borrowed straight from econometrics (Bai-Perron)
Inventing a bespoke 'lagging-trend' indicator (Dynamic Influencer Indicator) to capture leadership: an artist counts if their features predict the genre's trend one year ahead

Triangulation: confirming the DP change point (1950s) independently with boxplots of energy/acousticness across old vs new genres, and confirming revolutionaries via a separate Bayesian network of 1950s artists
Closing the loop from quantitative findings back to named real-world events (countercultural movement, Internet proliferation) to satisfy the 'cultural influence' narrative ask
why_it_won: From a judge's view this paper wins on breadth-with-rigor and a unifying spine. Every one of the 7 tasks gets a named, appropriate model rather than ad-hoc reasoning, and the same few primitives (a similarity measure, a network, a hypothesis test) are reused coherently across tasks so the paper reads as one integrated system, not seven disconnected mini-answers. Crucially, the team backs claims with real statistical tests and reports concrete numbers (62.8% / p<0.001 for genre similarity; p>0.1 for contagiousness; 72% vs 28% true-vs-fake followers) instead of eyeballing — judges reward defensible inference. It also handles the hard-to-quantify, judgment-call parts of an ICM problem (what is a 'revolution'? who is a 'revolutionary'? what is 'contagious'?) by giving each a crisp operational definition before modeling. The DP change-point result is independently corroborated (boxplots + a second Bayesian network identifying Elvis Presley/Cliff Richard), which signals robustness. There is a real sensitivity analysis on the trimming parameter tau showing the 1950 break is stable, an honest strengths/weaknesses section, and the required 'Document to the ICM Society' is delivered in accessible non-technical language. The combination of computer-science / network primitives with serious statistics is exactly the ICM sweet spot.
section_highlights: Strong, navigable structure: a Summary that gives a one-paragraph result for each of the 7 tasks up front; a Problem Restatement that re-articulates each task and states 'the key to this problem is...' before modeling; a workflow diagram (Fig 1) showing how datasets flow into models; an Assumptions section that justifies each assumption (e.g., 'artist similarity = similarity of their tracks' features'); a Notations table. Each task follows a consistent template — Definition/Setup, Model, Solutions with a figure — making it easy for a judge to find the contribution. Heavy, varied visualization (3D network, subnetwork radial graphs, similarity heatmaps, classification-tree diagram, genre-evolution bar charts, change-point line plots, boxplots, dynamic-influencer timeline) carries the results. The paper explicitly defends method choices in prose ('comparing to two-sample t-test, Mann-Whitney does not require distributional assumptions'), which reads as mathematical maturity. Sensitivity analysis, an honest Weaknesses section, and the plain-language ICM Society memo close it out.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Many of the embedded data tables/figures in this markdown are clearly OCR/extraction artifacts (monotone ramp values, repeated rows), so the rendered numbers in the heatmaps and some charts are not the paper's real values — the modeling text is reliable, the scraped chart data is not

Weights in the F-Score (w1=w2=w3=1/3) and the WDC weight scheme (1/3 increments, yeargap threshold=20) are chosen by hand with no optimization or justification beyond intuition — a judge could question the arbitrariness
Inverse-Euclidean-distance 'similarity' is undefined/explosive for identical tracks (division by ~0) and is not a standard similarity; ad-hoc though serviceable
Coverage is narrowed for convenience — Tasks 3-6 mostly drill into Pop/Rock and R&B rather than all genres (acknowledged as a weakness)

The piecewise-linear-trend + white-noise assumption for change-point detection is acknowledged to be unrealistic (real series are weakly-dependent, not iid), which weakens the revolution-dating inference
The contagiousness conclusion (no characteristic more contagious) rests on only 4 followers of one band (The Beatles); small n and single-influencer focus limits generality

Causal claims linking feature changes to events (Internet -> Electronic, countercultural movement) are narrative/post-hoc, supported by Wikipedia rather than modeling — appropriate for the prompt but not rigorous
Bayesian-network similarity edges are built on a Pearson-like correlation of feature vectors, conflating 'correlated feature profiles' with 'influence', which can produce spurious edges
reusable_patterns: Multi-metric fusion for a vague quantity: define 2-4 interpretable sub-metrics (each capturing one facet), min-max normalize, weighted-sum into a single rankable score — directly reusable for any 'rank/measure importance' OR task
Encode problem semantics into network edge weights (here: cross-category bonus + temporal-lag bonus) instead of treating all edges equally
Always PCA / decorrelate features before computing distances or similarities when features are obviously collinear

Default to non-parametric or asymptotic tests (Mann-Whitney, multivariate Chi-squared mean test) and explicitly justify why over t-test/Mahalanobis when normality is dubious — and mention BH correction when doing many tests

For 'when did the regime change?' questions, use DP change-point detection on a piecewise-trend model with an RSS objective, a min-segment trimming constraint, and a BIC penalty on the number of breaks (Bai-Perron) — clean, exact, O(T^2)
When exact structure search is NP-hard, state it, then use Hill-Climbing + random restarts scored by BIC and bootstrap-average many solutions for stability

Operationally define the squishy words in the prompt ('revolution', 'revolutionary', 'contagious', 'dynamic influencer') with a formula or threshold BEFORE modeling — this is what makes ICM answers defensible
Triangulate a key finding with a second independent method (DP change point confirmed by cross-genre boxplots and by a separate Bayesian network) so it doesn't hinge on one model
Build a custom leading-indicator metric using a time lag (compare an entity's value at t to the population trend at t-v) to identify who 'leads' a trend

Paper scaffolding that scores well: per-task Summary paragraphs, a dataset->model workflow diagram, justified assumptions table, consistent Definition/Model/Solution template per task, sensitivity analysis on at least one key parameter, honest weaknesses, and the required stakeholder memo in plain language
